utterances: I don't think I've ever said that to another guest.
(SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER)
Bill, thanks so much
You're most welcome.
The crisis in the Middle East is overshadowing the G8 Summit of the leading industrial countries in Russia this weekend.
NPR's Gregory Feifer is in St. Petersburg for the summit.
And Gregory, what are President Bush and the other G8 leaders saying about this crisis?
White House spokesman Tony Snow told reporters here that Mr. Bush has promised the Lebanese prime minister he'd press Israel to minimize civilian casualties.
But Mr. Bush won't urge the Israelis to stop their military operation.
Russian President Vladimir Putin, who is hosting the summit, says Israel's actions are unacceptable.
He said we assume that all sides in the conflict should immediately end their military action.
That should be the starting point for resolving all other problems.
Well, this isn't exactly how the Russians wanted things to go this weekend.
Is it?
Not at all.
Moscow is very much in the spotlight, as it hosts its first G8 Summit since becoming a member.
Putin had wanted energy security to top the meeting's agenda.
Also on the list are infectious diseases and education.
But it now appears the Middle East crisis will hang over the proceedings here.
Officials say the summit will include an emergency meeting to discuss the issue, with Moscow pushing for a strong statement over the crisis.
It's also certain that the topic will be part of Mr. Bush's private meetings with Putin on the sidelines of the summit today and tomorrow.
And as you just mentioned, he did arrive ahead of time to hold these private meetings.
What's on the agenda?
I think they're likely to find some agreement about Israel's military operations.
They'll also probably talk about other countries, like Iraq and Iran.
On Iran, Russia is against Washington's push to enact sanctions over Tehran's nuclear program.
Moscow has also been a strong critic of the American-led war in Iraq.
And how are the two leaders getting along publicly?
There was a minor (unintelligible) with Putin criticizing Cheney, and the president laughing that off.
So how are they getting along publicly?
Well, that's right.
I mean, there have been spats fired from a distance.
But Bush and Putin get along quite well when they're together.
They're very friendly.
They've both recently publicly reiterated their respect for one another and their friendship.
But there are issues that divide them.
Washington has criticized Russia's energy policy, saying Moscow is using its oil and gas supplies as a political tool.
Mr. Bush has also said he'll raise concerns about Moscow's turn away from democracy when he meets Putin.
Today, Mr. Bush also met with human rights and youth groups, which probably irked the Kremlin.
NPR's Gregory Feifer in St. Petersburg for the G8 Summit.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
Matthew Hughey is an African-American studies professor at the University of Connecticut.
He says schools don't suddenly become inclusive after a long history of segregation.
Having black women pledge sororities at the University of Alabama is a step.
But he says it may be largely a symbolic one unless inequities are addressed across the board.

Until equal resources are afforded to all of the Greek organizations on campus in terms of housing, in terms of advisers, in terms of the culture of the university, and in recruiting students and in retaining students and so forth, anything less to me is window dressing and is homage to political correctness, and trying to make this issue go out of the media cycle.
Others are hopeful.
Cleo Thomas Jr. is a lawyer in Anniston, Ala., and was the only black student to be elected president of the student government association on the Tuscaloosa campus.
He says it's hard to know what's really happening because the sorority recruiting process is so secretive.
The success of this will not necessarily be measured, going forward, by the numbers.
There'll have to be a deeper analysis than that.
Thomas says the issue is whether students who want to join, and can afford to join, are ultimately accepted.
I think what there is a - absolute insistence upon is that the humanity of black students be acknowledged, and they be free to make such associations as are mutually agreeable.
The removal of barriers is the right thing to do.
It's the only thing to do.
The university president has promised more diversity.
The faculty senate met this week and is creating a task force to address segregation; and a new student group has vowed to keep the pressure on.
Kathy Lohr, NPR News.
As the war in Iraq enters its fourth year, the U.S.
Army is struggling to find new soldiers.
The Army has reached its monthly recruiting goals so far this year, but an internal memo obtained by NPR says that the Army could be short as many as 7,000 recruits by the end of the year.
NPR's Tom Bowman has the story.
Sergeant Gilbert Rosa[ph] flipped through a binder, thick with the names of high school seniors.
He punches in a home phone number and begins his pitch.
(On the telephone) Hello, how are you doing, ma'am?
This is Sergeant Rosa.
I'm a U.S.
Army recruiter for Hilton High School.
Is ...
Sergeant Rosa is luckier than most recruiters.
This storefront recruiting station in the Virginia suburbs has been making its quota, 13 or 14 teenagers each month.
Nationwide, it's a different story.
Documents obtained by NPR show that the active duty Army is projected to miss its goal of 80,000 recruits by 7,000 this fall.
Last year the Army was also 7,000 below its needs, the largest shortfall since 1979 and the first time in six years that the Army failed to make its recruiting target.
It is more challenging in this environment because we are in sustained combat operations in Afghanistan and Iraq.
That's Major General Tom Bostick, a West Point graduate who heads the Army's Recruiting Command.
He brushed aside the projections, and said he still hopes to recruit 80,000 by year's end.
The models we have here do not include the incentives, the motivation of our recruiters, and really any changes in policies.
So whatever shortages, let's take your number that you gave, 7,000, I have over 7,000 recruiters out there right now.
It's an increase of 1,200.
But if each recruiter wrote one extra contract, that makes up the 7,000.
But some retired generals and lawmakers fear that if the Army is once again thousands of recruits below its goal, the impact could be serious.
Combat units would be increasingly under strength.
The Army would have to move more soldiers around to fill those units heading for Iraq.
It may have to cannibalize important Army functions, such as training, to fill the gaps.
Retired Lieutenant General Ted Stroop was in charge of Army personnel during the 1991 Gulf War.

And the real question, which we will not be able to answer until you really see whether or not the Army makes its final recruiting marks and that would be perhaps four to five months after the recruiting year is closed, is whether or not you will find shortages in some of the combat units.
In the institutional and the training army, there's liable to be some critical skill shortages in the mid-ranks.
Others say the Army may be forced to lower standards still further, to pick up more recruits.
The Army already has doubled the number of recruits with the lowest scores on military aptitude tests.
Rhode Island Senator Jack Reed, a former Army paratrooper, fears the quality of the force will continue to drop.
Every year that this gap exists, there's much more pressure to lower the standards.
And Reed says with fewer recruits, the Army may also find it difficult to increase the number of combat brigades.
You might even have to start making some concessions when it comes to force structure.
You might not be able to fill all the new modular brigades if you don't have adequate, you know, enlisted personnel.
The Army wants to create 42 of these combat units in the next few years, up from the current 33.
Soldiers would then have fewer deployments to Iraq.
Andrew Krepinevich, a retired Army officer and defense analyst, worries about the long-term effect of the recruiting shortfall on the country's largest military service.
The thing that no one really knows is, where's the red line?
You know, where does the Army as an institution really begin to hit the wall?
Where does the Army become progressively less combat effective?
But General Bostick of the Recruiting Command is confident his soldiers will meet the mark by year's end.
There are more recruiters on the street, and a bigger ad budget.
Andy says the Army's considering a few more incentives to attract recruits: loans to buy a house, or start a small business.
In the meantime, Sergeant Rosa and his fellow recruiters continue to work the phones in suburban Virginia.
(On the telephone) As you already know, the U.S.
Army, we have over 200 jobs available.
And you know we have ultimate ...
Tom Bowman, NPR News, Washington.
Now, we've got a closer look at prosperity gospel with Reverend Stephan Epps.
He's a professor of religion and philosophy at Medgar Evers College in Brooklyn.
So, professor, let's listen to little bit of Creflo Dollar talking about God and money.
Sure.
(Soundbite of recorded audio)
See, it's easy for a person to sit back who ain't never been there.
It's easy for you to say that God don't want you rich.
But for some folks, God is the only way out of their luck-poverty situation, and He is the father that watches you all in every area of your life.
But He also wants you to have money in your pocketbook, so you can have authority in this world.
That's compelling.
No doubt.
What's wrong with it, if anything?

Well, if we can sort of unmuddy the waters for your listeners, the prosperity gospel posits that financial and material wealth is perhaps an external sign that God is favoring graces on the believers' life.
In the theological world, that does raise some eyebrows because the question is then for what reason do we worship and praise God?
And never forget that Roger L'Estrange said he who serves God for money will serve the devil for better wages.
So, it seems to me that the prosperity gospel is ultimately trying to figure out is God - to what degree is God complicit in the success and failure of my life?
And that's a very important question to ask theologically, and I don't know if there's anything necessarily wrong with that particular discussion.
